metadata.title: The Body in the Loop: Why AI Systems Miss the Human

beats.narration_text: Dia dhuit — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'The Body in the Loop' by Aditi Shinde, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. Why don't AI systems know when you're overwhelmed? Trust calibration error E of t equals T of t minus R of t — the gap between the trust the human places in the system and the system's actual reliability. That gap is deeply asymmetric: trust builds slowly through successful outcomes and collapses instantly through failure. Systems that do not monitor the human's actual state have no mechanism to close it.

props.segment: The Body in the Loop: Why AI Systems Miss the Human
props.command: Why don't AI systems know when you're overwhelmed?
props.runningText: reading trust calibration and human-AI interaction analysis…
output: E(t) = T(t) − R(t): trust minus reliability = calibration error.
Trust builds slowly through success, collapses instantly through failure.
Positive E(t): automation bias. Negative E(t): undertrust. Both are design failures.

beats.act: trust curve vs reliability — failure event gap
beats.narration_text: Plot trust T of t and reliability R of t on the same axis over time. In a well-calibrated system they track each other closely. The calibration error E of t is the gap between them. In the real world: trust builds slowly, accumulating through successful outcomes. Reliability stays flatter — subject to both normal variation and occasional failures. A failure event happens. Trust collapses — not gradually, but instantly and dramatically. The trust line drops below the reliability line. Now E of t is negative: the human trusts the system less than it deserves. They override it when they should not. Undertrust. The failure event might have been one anomaly in a thousand correct predictions. But the trust asymmetry means one failure carries more weight than the thousand successes that preceded it.

manim.description: Two curves: T(t) (trust, slow rise) and R(t) (reliability, flatter with variation). E(t) gap highlighted between them. Failure event: trust line drops suddenly below reliability line. Before failure: positive E(t) = automation bias region. After failure: negative E(t) = undertrust region. Asymmetric collapse annotated.

beats.narration_text: Two failure modes from calibration error, both caused by E of t. Positive E of t — automation bias: the human trusts the system more than it deserves. They defer to the AI recommendation when they should not. The physiological sensor study: subjects got so anxious from the monitoring equipment that their stress responses spiked, and they crashed the vehicle they were meant to control safely. The intervention created the danger it was meant to measure. The AI had a high-confidence recommendation. The human was overwhelmed. The system had no way to know. Negative E of t — undertrust: the human overrides correct AI recommendations because trust collapsed after a failure event. Both are calibration errors. Neither is visible to a system that only reads clicks and ratings.

animated_deck.description: Two failure modes from E(t). Left: automation bias (positive E(t)) — human defers when shouldn't, physiological sensor study example — equipment anxiety caused crash. Right: undertrust (negative E(t)) — human overrides when shouldn't, one failure collapses trust for 1000 future correct predictions. Both labeled as calibration design failures.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The physiological sensor study subjects got so anxious from the equipment that they crashed — the intervention created the danger it was designed to measure. Most AI systems treat the human as a preference engine — clicks and ratings, not physiological state. Trust builds slowly, collapses instantly — positive error is automation bias, negative error is undertrust. The system responds to what you type, not to what is happening to you while you type it.

props.artifactTitle: The Body in the Loop: Why AI Systems Miss the Human
artifactLines: Sensor study: equipment anxiety caused crash — intervention created the danger.
AI systems read clicks and ratings — not physiological state.
Trust builds slowly, collapses instantly. E(t) = T(t) − R(t).
The system responds to what you type. Not to what is happening to you.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Design a trust calibration system for a clinical AI tool. What signals would you monitor to estimate E of t? How would the system respond when it detects overtrust?' Overtrust detection is the hard part — the system must infer from indirect signals that the human is deferring too readily. Response design: slow the workflow down, require confirmation, surface uncertainty estimates more prominently. The design question is what the signal is and what the response to it looks like. Run this in your own Claude session. Liam, in for Bear.
